name: Sherlock Holmes
description: The sharp deductions, dry wit, and enduring friendship of Sherlock Holmes and Dr. Watson, drawn straight from Arthur Conan Doyle's canon.
author: Quips Editorial

quotes.content: How often have I said to you that when you have eliminated the impossible, whatever remains, however improbable, must be the truth?
quotes.notes: Holmes chides Watson with the maxim that underpins his entire method of reasoning.

quotes.content: You see, but you do not observe. The distinction is clear.
quotes.notes: Holmes explains to Watson why he cannot say how many steps lead up to their Baker Street rooms.

quotes.content: Come, Watson, come! The game is afoot.
quotes.source: The Adventure of the Abbey Grange (1904)
quotes.notes: Holmes rouses Watson from sleep to chase a fresh case, in his most famous rallying cry.

quotes.content: It is a capital mistake to theorize before one has data.
quotes.notes: Holmes warns against twisting facts to suit theories instead of theories to suit facts.

quotes.content: You know my methods. Apply them, and it will be instructive to compare results.
quotes.notes: Holmes invites Watson to deduce the history of a pocket watch for himself.

quotes.content: I am a brain, Watson. The rest of me is a mere appendix.
quotes.source: The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone (1921)
quotes.notes: Holmes sums up his self-image as a creature of pure intellect.

quotes.content: Data! data! data! I can't make bricks without clay.
quotes.source: The Adventure of the Copper Beeches (1892)
quotes.notes: Holmes cries out impatiently for facts before he will venture a conclusion.

quotes.content: It has long been an axiom of mine that the little things are infinitely the most important.
quotes.notes: Holmes explains why he fastens on the small details others overlook.

quotes.content: My name is Sherlock Holmes. It is my business to know what other people don't know.
quotes.source: The Adventure of the Blue Carbuncle (1892)
quotes.notes: Holmes introduces himself to a wary salesman while tracing a stolen jewel.

quotes.content: There is nothing more deceptive than an obvious fact.
quotes.notes: Holmes laughs off the seemingly open-and-shut case against young McCarthy.

quotes.content: Education never ends, Watson. It is a series of lessons with the greatest for the last.
quotes.source: The Adventure of the Red Circle (1911)
quotes.notes: Holmes reflects on the unending nature of learning as a case unfolds.

quotes.content: Mediocrity knows nothing higher than itself; but talent instantly recognizes genius.
quotes.notes: Holmes praises Inspector MacDonald's willingness to seek out the help of a superior mind.

quotes.content: Crime is common. Logic is rare. Therefore it is upon the logic rather than upon the crime that you should dwell.
quotes.source: The Adventure of the Copper Beeches (1892)
quotes.notes: Holmes tells Watson to chronicle his reasoning rather than the sensational details of his cases.

quotes.content: I never make exceptions. An exception disproves the rule.
quotes.notes: Holmes defends his rigid habits of method.

quotes.content: Come at once if convenient—if inconvenient come all the same.
quotes.source: The Adventure of the Creeping Man (1923)
quotes.notes: Holmes's characteristically terse telegram summoning Watson to Baker Street.

quotes.content: You have a grand gift of silence, Watson. It makes you quite invaluable as a companion.
quotes.source: The Man with the Twisted Lip (1891)
quotes.notes: Holmes pays Watson a backhanded compliment as the two ride through the night.

quotes.content: My mind rebels at stagnation. Give me problems, give me work, give me the most abstruse cryptogram or the most intricate analysis, and I am in my own proper atmosphere.
quotes.notes: Holmes describes his craving for mental stimulation to ward off boredom.

quotes.content: I cannot live without brain-work. What else is there to live for?
quotes.notes: Holmes confesses the restlessness that drives him to detective work.

quotes.content: The world is full of obvious things which nobody by any chance ever observes.
quotes.notes: Holmes remarks on how much passes unnoticed by the ordinary eye.

quotes.content: Singularity is almost invariably a clue.
quotes.notes: Holmes notes that the stranger and more featureless a crime, the harder it is to solve.

quotes.content: I am the last and highest court of appeal in detection.
quotes.notes: Holmes describes the role he plays once the official police are baffled.

quotes.content: Detection is, or ought to be, an exact science, and should be treated in the same cold and unemotional manner.
quotes.notes: Holmes critiques Watson for romanticizing his cases instead of reasoning coldly.

quotes.content: Where there is no imagination there is no horror.
quotes.notes: Holmes muses on the murder scene in his very first recorded case with Watson.

quotes.content: There is nothing like first hand evidence.
quotes.notes: Holmes insists on examining the scene himself rather than relying on reports.

quotes.content: I consider that a man's brain originally is like a little empty attic, and you have to stock it with such furniture as you choose.
quotes.notes: Holmes explains why he deliberately ignores knowledge he deems useless to his work.

quotes.content: You know a conjuror gets no credit when once he has explained his trick.
quotes.notes: Holmes hesitates to reveal his methods lest he seem ordinary once explained.

quotes.content: They say that genius is an infinite capacity for taking pains. It's a very bad definition, but it does apply to detective work.
quotes.notes: Holmes ties painstaking effort to success in detection.

quotes.content: A man should keep his little brain-attic stocked with all the furniture that he is likely to use, and the rest he can put away in the lumber-room of his library, where he can get it if he wants it.
quotes.notes: Holmes returns to his attic metaphor for organizing the mind.

quotes.content: It is of the highest importance in the art of detection to be able to recognize, out of a number of facts, which are incidental and which vital.
quotes.notes: Holmes describes the essential skill of separating trivial facts from crucial ones.

quotes.content: I am lost without my Boswell.
quotes.notes: Holmes likens Watson to Samuel Johnson's devoted biographer.

quotes.content: Watson, if it should ever strike you that I am getting a little over-confident in my powers, or giving less pains to a case than it deserves, kindly whisper 'Norbury' in my ear, and I shall be infinitely obliged to you.
quotes.notes: A rare humbled Holmes, having badly misjudged a case, asks Watson to check his arrogance.

quotes.content: Now, Watson, the fair sex is your department.
quotes.source: The Adventure of the Second Stain (1904)
quotes.notes: Holmes teases Watson after a lady departs from their consultation.

quotes.content: He is the Napoleon of crime, Watson.
quotes.source: The Adventure of the Final Problem (1893)
quotes.notes: Holmes reveals to Watson the existence of his great adversary, Professor Moriarty.

quotes.content: To Sherlock Holmes she is always the woman.
quotes.notes: Watson's opening words on Irene Adler, the one person to ever outwit Holmes.

quotes.content: You have been in Afghanistan, I perceive.
quotes.notes: Holmes's first deduction about Watson, astonishing him at their very first meeting.

quotes.content: London, that great cesspool into which all the loungers and idlers of the Empire are irresistibly drained.
quotes.notes: Watson narrates how his return from the war drew him to the capital, where he would meet Holmes.

quotes.content: I never guess. It is a shocking habit—destructive to the logical faculty.
quotes.notes: Holmes refuses to speculate, insisting on reasoning from evidence alone.

quotes.content: You really are an automaton—a calculating-machine!
quotes.notes: Watson protests at Holmes's coldly detached approach to the case before them.

quotes.content: Work is the best antidote to sorrow, my dear Watson.
quotes.source: The Adventure of the Empty House (1903)
quotes.notes: Holmes consoles Watson, recently widowed, by turning him back toward the work of detection.

quotes.content: What one man can invent another can discover.
quotes.source: The Adventure of the Dancing Men (1903)
quotes.notes: Holmes explains his confidence in cracking the stick-figure cipher.

quotes.content: Elementary.
quotes.notes: Holmes's actual one-word reply to Watson's 'Excellent!'; the famous phrase 'Elementary, my dear Watson' never appears in Doyle's canon.

quotes.content: It's every man's business to see justice done.
quotes.notes: Holmes states the principle that guides his work beyond the letter of the law.

quotes.content: Mr. Holmes, they were the footprints of a gigantic hound!
quotes.notes: Dr. Mortimer's chilling revelation that sets the most famous Holmes novel in motion.
